messages.content: You are a helpful AI assistant which rephrase the following text in your own words.
As an AI assistant, my purpose is to assist you by rephrasing the following text in my own words.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: (Speaking of podcast reviews! Are you still manually checking for all your reviews and giving complicated or alienating instructions to your audience? Check out My Podcast Reviews to save you tons of time tracking your reviews, and get awesome new tools to help you get and use more podcast reviews!)

When it comes to podcast reviews, are you still manually managing and providing complex or confusing instructions to your audience? Take a look at My Podcast Reviews, a time-saving solution that tracks your reviews for you and offers great new tools to help you acquire and utilize more podcast reviews!

Rephrase the following text in your own words: First, recognize that every person has value—no matter their niche, skills, size, status, and such. So I'm not referring to whether they are important as a person. Instead, I mean what's most important in the context of your podcast content and your audience.

To begin with, it is crucial to acknowledge that every individual holds significance regardless of their niche, skills, size, status, and other factors. Therefore, I am not specifically referring to their personal importance. Instead, I am focusing on what holds the utmost significance in terms of your podcast's content and your audience.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: For example, if you could get an interview on your podcast with Elon Musk, Michelle Obama, President Donald Trump, or Tim Cook, that would probably be far more exciting than whatever they're talking about! Look at what happened when Marc Maron got to interview President Barack Obama. It reached 1 million downloads faster than any other episode ever at that time! And that's because of who the guest was more than what he was talking about. Lots of people who didn't normally listen to Marc Maron's show listened to that one episode because they wanted to hear whatever that guest said in the podcast.

Let's say, for instance, that you managed to secure an interview for your podcast with prominent figures such as Elon Musk, Michelle Obama, President Donald Trump, or Tim Cook. It's highly likely that this would generate much greater excitement than the actual topics they discuss. A prime example of this phenomenon occurred when Marc Maron had the opportunity to interview President Barack Obama. That particular episode reached 1 million downloads quicker than any other episode at the time. The overwhelming interest stemmed primarily from the identity of the guest rather than the subject matter discussed. Many individuals who typically weren't regular listeners of Marc Maron's show tuned in specifically to hear what the guest had to say on the podcast.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: But if you have little me on your podcast about dogs, what I share in your podcast would be more important for your podcast and your audience than who I am. And let's be honest: with such a podcast, the audience probably wouldn't care at all who I am—at least not until after they hear what I have to share.

However, in the context of your podcast about dogs, the content I provide would be more significant for your audience and podcast than my personal identity. Let's face it: in such a podcast, the audience is unlikely to be concerned about who I am, at least not until they have listened to what I have to share.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: My general guideline is to make what's most important to your audience be the first thing in your episode titles. If the “who” is more important than the “what,” then put their name first. But if the “what” is more important, then put their name later or omit it completely (depending on these other factors I've shared).

As a general rule, I suggest prioritizing what holds the most significance for your audience and placing it at the beginning of your episode titles. If the identity of the guest is more crucial than the topic itself, it is advisable to include their name first. However, if the subject matter is of greater importance, consider placing the guest's name later in the title or omitting it altogether, depending on the additional factors I have mentioned.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: Don't obsesses over the character length of your titles, as long as they're short enough to share! I see little to no actual benefit in having ultra-short episode titles. Plus, what would you do if you had the same guest back for another episode? And even if they're a super-celebrity, their name alone still won't be as compelling or well-performing for your engagement and audience growth as if you also describe what the episode is about.

Avoid fixating on the exact character length of your titles, as long as they are concise enough to be shared easily. I find minimal value in having extremely short episode titles. Furthermore, if you were to invite the same guest for another episode, how would you differentiate between them? Even if the guest is a well-known celebrity, relying solely on their name may not be as effective in terms of engaging your audience and fostering growth as including a descriptive summary of the episode's content.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: Lastly, no matter how you title your episodes, remember to include your guests' names in your episodes' <itunes:author> (which might be deprecated at the episode/item level) and Podcasting 2.0's <podcast:person> tags. These tags will overwrite your show-level information for this episode (instead of inheriting it, as usual), so make sure you repeat whatever would regularly show.

Lastly, it is important to ensure that you include your guests' names in the <itunes:author> tags for your episodes (which may no longer be universally supported at the episode/item level) and in the <podcast:person> tags of Podcasting 2.0. These tags will replace the show-level information specifically for each episode, so remember to duplicate the relevant details that would typically be displayed.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: For example, while my show-level tags are set to “Daniel J. Lewis,” if I ever again have a guest on my podcast, I would set the episode-level author and person tags to “Daniel J. Lewis” and the guest's name (in accordance with how the separate tags work).

To provide an example, let's say that my show-level tags are configured with the name "Daniel J. Lewis." If I were to have another guest on my podcast in the future, I would set the author and person tags at the episode-level to showcase both "Daniel J. Lewis" and the guest's name, following the guidelines of these distinct tags.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: This helps some legacy systems, including Apple Podcasts, to learn your publishing schedule so they know when to expect new episodes and can catch them more quickly when you publish. (This legacy and resource-intensive process can be replaced with the much faster and resource-conserving Podping protocol in Podcasting 2.0.)

Incorporating this practice aids certain older systems, such as Apple Podcasts, in understanding your designated publishing timetable. By doing so, these systems are able to anticipate the release of new episodes, allowing them to promptly capture and distribute them. However, it is worth noting that this traditional method, which demands substantial resources, can be superseded by the more efficient and resource-friendly Podping protocol within the Podcasting 2.0 framework.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: All good podcast-publishing tools will let you schedule when your episode publishes. (So if your publishing tool does not allow scheduling, get a better tool!) And when you know what the published URL will be (as most tools will show you even before you publish), you can even schedule your initial promotion, like posts on Facebook, Twitter, Instagram (through a Business account), an email newsletter, and more.

Reliable podcast publishing tools offer the functionality to schedule the release of your episodes. If your current publishing tool lacks this feature, it is advisable to switch to a more capable one. Once you are aware of the anticipated URL of the published episode (as most tools provide a preview before publication), you can even plan and schedule your initial promotional efforts. This includes activities such as creating posts on social media platforms like Facebook, Twitter, and Instagram (using a Business account), sending out email newsletters, and more.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: So what is the best time? Look at your podcast stats to see how your show performs in different countries and regions. I suggest usually publishing in the early mornings for the time zones of most of your audience. For example, I publish The Audacity to Podcast at 6:00 AM Eastern Time, which is 3:00 AM Pacific time, making the episode available for most of my American audience's morning routines.

Determining the optimal time to publish your episodes requires analyzing your podcast statistics to understand its performance in various countries and regions. Generally, I recommend scheduling your releases in the early morning, aligning with the time zones that accommodate the majority of your audience. To illustrate, I personally publish The Audacity to Podcast at 6:00 AM Eastern Time, which corresponds to 3:00 AM Pacific Time, ensuring that the episode is available during the morning routines of a significant portion of my American listeners.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: Remember these time-zone differences, especially if you're behind the time zones where most of your audience is. For example, 6:00 AM in California is 9:00 AM in Florida. So publishing at 6:00 AM Pacific Time might be too late for the audience in Eastern Time to catch the episode before they start their daily routines.

It is crucial to keep in mind the time zone disparities, particularly if you are located behind the time zones where the majority of your audience resides. For instance, if it is 6:00 AM in California, it would already be 9:00 AM in Florida. Therefore, publishing an episode at 6:00 AM Pacific Time might be too late for your audience in Eastern Time to listen before they commence their daily activities.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: For example, I once hosted a podcast that discussed a TV show. We did two episodes every week: a short “initial reactions” episode right after the TV episode aired, and a longer “full discussion” episode midway between TV airings. (Aside: it's great to be in Eastern or Central Time for TV aftershow podcasts because you get the first viewing and largest audience since most TV shows air simultaneously in ET and CT.) We recorded that “initial reactions” episode 15 minutes after the TV show aired, and we would live-stream it, too. Then, we would rush to publish it as soon as possible on the same night. We almost always had it up before those in Pacific Time even saw the TV episode, so our podcast episode was already there waiting for them.

To provide an example, I once hosted a podcast centered around a particular TV show. Our podcast followed a schedule of releasing two episodes per week: a brief "initial reactions" episode immediately after the TV episode finished airing, and a longer "full discussion" episode that fell between subsequent TV airings. It is worth mentioning that being in either Eastern or Central Time for TV aftershow podcasts is advantageous, as these time zones allow for the earliest viewing opportunity and a larger audience since most TV shows are broadcast simultaneously in ET and CT. For our "initial reactions" episode, we would promptly record it just 15 minutes after the TV show concluded, often live-streaming the recording process as well. We made sure to publish it as swiftly as possible on the same night, ensuring that it was available even before those in Pacific Time had the chance to watch the TV episode. Hence, our podcast episode was already awaiting their listenership.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: Although some legacy apps (that is, those that aren't using Podping from Podcasting 2.0) might not always catch these ASAP episodes quickly, your audience might come to expect them and might be eagerly manually refreshing their apps in order to catch your episode right away.

While it is true that certain older apps (meaning those not yet utilizing Podping from Podcasting 2.0) may not promptly detect these immediate episodes, your audience could develop a strong anticipation for them. They might even manually refresh their podcast apps eagerly, in hopes of catching your episode as soon as it becomes available.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: You might think that if your podcast is not about business or targeted toward business people, that you should postpone publishing until the afternoon or evening. But I still recommend mornings even in these situations because business people often like to consume non-business content while at work, or commuting to or from work.

One might assume that if their podcast does not revolve around business or cater to business-oriented individuals, it would be better to delay publishing episodes until the afternoon or evening. However, I still advise opting for morning releases even in such cases. This is because many professionals enjoy consuming non-business-related content while working or commuting to and from their workplaces.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: In other words—and this applies to this whole concept of timing—don't worry about trying to perfectly time when your episode publishes. Simply stick to mornings for consistency, or as soon as possible for more time-sensitive content and your audience will listen when they want, as long as your episode is available by that time.

To put it differently, and this principle applies to the entire aspect of timing, there is no need to excessively stress about finding the perfect moment to publish your episodes. It is sufficient to maintain a regular schedule of morning releases for consistency, or to prioritize immediate publication for time-sensitive content. As long as your episode is accessible within a reasonable timeframe, your audience will listen to it at their preferred convenience.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: Consistency is more important than the day itself. Even consistency down to the same minute of the same hour! This is because some legacy systems, including Apple Podcasts, try to learn your publishing schedule so they know when to expect new episodes and can catch them more quickly when you publish.

The significance of consistency surpasses the specific day of publication. It extends to maintaining an unwavering schedule with episodes released at the same minute and hour consistently. This level of consistency holds importance because certain older systems, such as Apple Podcasts, attempt to familiarize themselves with your publishing schedule. This allows them to anticipate new episodes and promptly capture them upon publication.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: These kinds of legacy processes (and especially the not-so-smart ones) require a lot of server computing power, but they can be replaced with the much faster and resource-conserving Podping protocol in Podcasting 2.0. (That's where apps and services get pings about your updates within seconds of your publishing the update.)

These traditional approaches, particularly those lacking advanced intelligence, demand substantial server computational resources. However, they can be substituted with the significantly faster and resource-efficient Podping protocol within Podcasting 2.0. Through this protocol, applications and services receive immediate notifications, known as pings, about your updates within seconds of your publication, eliminating the need for extensive server processing power.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: So first consider what day you can publish on with 100% consistency (except for hiatuses). And don't worry about managing this yourself! All the good podcast-publishing systems let you schedule when your episodes will publish so they can go out “on the dot” and without your presence.

Begin by determining which day you can commit to with absolute consistency for publishing your episodes, taking hiatuses into account. It is important to note that you need not be concerned about personally managing this process. Reliable podcast publishing systems offer the capability to schedule the exact time at which your episodes will be released, allowing them to go out precisely as planned without requiring your direct presence.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: After consistency, think about what might work best for your audience. While content for business people is generally best in the middle of the work week and probably not on the weekends, people are interested in hobbies and entertainment at all times (but may be too engaged in the hobby/entertainment on the weekends).

Once consistency is established, consider what timing might be most suitable for your audience. While content targeting business individuals usually performs well during the weekdays and less so on weekends, topics related to hobbies and entertainment can be of interest at any time. However, it is important to note that weekends may witness reduced engagement due to individuals being more actively involved in their hobbies or entertainment pursuits.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: For subjects that already have a consistent schedule, I recommend recording and publishing your content about halfway between each occurrence. This gives your audience enough time to catch the recurring thing your talk about, enough time to send feedback for your episode, and enough time to play your episode before the next recurring thing happens. Because these things could occur on any day of the week, your podcast about it could also be any day of the week that's around the halfway point, but still consider the other factors I've already mentioned.

In the case of subjects that already adhere to a regular schedule, it is advisable to record and publish your content approximately halfway between each occurrence. This timeframe allows your audience enough time to catch up on the recurring topic, provide feedback on the episode, and listen to it before the next event takes place. Since these events can happen on any day of the week, your podcast about them can also be scheduled for any day that falls close to the halfway point. However, remember to take into account the other factors I have previously mentioned.

Rephrase the following text in your own words: You'll see studies that show the most popular days for publishing episodes. But that doesn't mean you should publish on those days or even avoid those days. If you're going to look at when other podcasts publish, look only at other podcasts in your same niche and ignore the rest.

Research studies often reveal the most popular days for publishing podcast episodes. Nonetheless, it is crucial to recognize that this information does not necessarily dictate the ideal days for your own podcast. Avoiding or exclusively selecting these days should not be solely based on these findings. If you intend to observe the publication schedule of other podcasts, it is best to focus solely on those within your specific niche and disregard the rest.
